7.5.3 Output Files

As of 2026-05-07 the script reads SRK_individual_BL_assignments.tsv and applies a split-filter design: the species-level curve uses ALL ingroup individuals (so the species pool baseline is inclusive), while EO and BL curves use only the BL-assigned individuals. Current dataset (2026-05-11): species pool = 49 observed / MM = 59 / Chao1 = 61 / consensus = 60; species-level uses all 335 ingroup individuals, EO/BL curves use 325 BL-assigned (was 54 / MM = 69 / 272 species / 262 BL-assigned prior to Library 010 and Steps 4b/7b).

7.5.3.1 Primary Outputs

SRK_allele_accumulation_curves.pdf

Contains, on separate pages:

  • Species-level accumulation curve (all 335 ingroup individuals)
  • One page per EO with N ≥ 5 (ordered by parent BL connectivity → ascending mean Drift_index)
  • One page per BL (all 5 BLs, regardless of N, in connectivity order BL5→BL2)

Each page also overlays MM, Chao1, and (optional) iNEXT asymptote reference lines.

Standalone PNGs (in figures/)

  • figures/SRK_allele_accumulation_species.png — two-panel species figure: stacked bar (observed + predicted-undetected = MM) on the left, rarefaction curve with MM/Chao1/iNEXT asymptotes on the right, both sharing the bar’s y-axis. The bar uses the same dark-blue/light-blue colour key as the BL/EO drift-erosion bars, so the species, BL, and EO panels can be read together.
  • figures/presentation/SRK_allele_accumulation_species_observed.png / _predicted.png — slide build-up frames of the figure above: frame 1 shows only the bar’s observed segment (no curve, no MM line); frame 2 shows the full bar + curve + MM dashed line (Chao1/iNEXT suppressed). Same 12 × 6.5 in canvas across frames for clean animation.
  • figures/SRK_allele_accumulation_combined.png — all qualifying EO curves on shared axes, colored by parent BL (Set1 palette). End-of-curve labels show EOXX (observed/MM). Legend grouped by BL.
  • figures/SRK_allele_accumulation_BL_combined.png — NEW. The 5 BL aggregate curves on shared axes; the headline visual test of “are independent bottleneck lineages approaching saturation independently?” Headline finding (current dataset 2026-05-11): BL4 climbs to 27 alleles toward MM=37 while BL2 plateaus at 8 alleles (MM=10).
  • figures/SRK_allele_accumulation_drift_erosion.png — stacked bars per EO (observed / predicted-undetected / lost-to-drift), with EOs sorted by parent BL and a thin BL color strip along the x-axis baseline grouping EOs visually.
  • figures/SRK_allele_accumulation_BL_drift_erosion.png — NEW. Same decomposition aggregated to the BL level. Headline finding: BL4 has lost only 32% of the species pool; BL1 (~90%) and BL2 (~86%) have collapsed.

SRK_allele_accumulation_stats.tsv

Tabulated statistics extracted from each curve. Now contains rows for Species, then 6 EO rows (sorted by parent BL), then 5 BL rows.

Column Description
Level Species, EO, or BL
Population Group identifier (All, EO27, BL2, …)
N_individuals Number of individuals in the analysis (272 for Species; 262-subset for EO/BL)
N_alleles Observed alleles
Initial_slope Mean allele gain per individual in early sampling
Tail_slope Mean allele gain per individual in late sampling
Late_fraction Proportion of diversity contributed by rare alleles
MM_estimate Michaelis-Menten asymptote estimate
Chao1_estimate Chao1 non-parametric richness estimate
iNEXT_estimate iNEXT asymptotic estimate (NA if package not installed)
N_more_next_allele Additional individuals needed to discover one more allele beyond observed
N_more_80pct_MM Additional individuals needed to reach 80% of the MM asymptote
N_more_90pct_MM Additional individuals needed to reach 90% of the MM asymptote
N_more_95pct_MM Additional individuals needed to reach 95% of the MM asymptote
N_more_99pct_MM Additional individuals needed to reach 99% of the MM asymptote

Example (current dataset, 2026-05-07):

Level Population N_ind N_alleles MM Chao1
Species All 272 54 69 73
EO EO70 48 6 7 8
EO EO27 42 22 38 26
EO EO25 53 14 17 15
BL BL1 5 4 7 5
BL BL2 51 8 10 11
BL BL3 68 16 19 29
BL BL4 78 31 47 40
BL BL5 60 18 22 21

SRK_species_richness_estimates.tsv

A dedicated summary of species-level richness estimates used as the NFDS optimum in downstream frequency analyses.

Column Description
S_observed Observed allele count across all ingroup individuals
MM_estimate Michaelis-Menten asymptote
Chao1_estimate Chao1 lower-bound estimate
iNEXT_estimate iNEXT asymptotic estimate
Consensus_estimate Mean of all available estimators
N_more_next_allele Additional individuals needed to discover one more allele beyond observed
N_more_80pct_MM Additional individuals needed to reach 80% of the MM asymptote
N_more_90pct_MM Additional individuals needed to reach 90% of the MM asymptote
N_more_95pct_MM Additional individuals needed to reach 95% of the MM asymptote
N_more_99pct_MM Additional individuals needed to reach 99% of the MM asymptote

7.5.4 Biological Interpretation

Three statistics extracted from accumulation curves provide insight into the evolutionary forces shaping allele diversity.

7.5.4.1 Initial Slope

Measures how rapidly new alleles appear early in sampling.

Initial_slope > 1   → high rare allele diversity
Initial_slope < 0.5 → few dominant alleles

High values are expected under negative frequency-dependent selection.

7.5.4.2 Tail Slope

Measures whether new alleles continue appearing late in sampling.

Tail_slope ≈ 0 → curve saturation (drift)
Tail_slope > 0.2 → ongoing rare allele discovery (NFDS)

Persistent discovery of alleles is characteristic of balancing selection.

7.5.4.3 Late Fraction

Quantifies how much allelic diversity is composed of rare alleles appearing late in sampling.

Late_fraction =
(alleles_total − alleles_half) / alleles_total

Interpretation:

Late_fraction > 0.30 → strong NFDS
Late_fraction < 0.10 → drift-dominated diversity

This metric captures the proportion of diversity contributed by rare alleles.

7.5.5 Evolutionary Interpretation Framework

Comparing species-level and population-level statistics provides insight into evolutionary dynamics.

Pattern Interpretation
High species slopes, low population slopes Migration + local drift
High slopes at both levels Strong NFDS maintaining diversity
Low slopes at both levels Genetic drift dominates

This framework complements traditional neutrality tests by directly analyzing allele discovery dynamics.

7.5.6 Bioinformatics

  • Script: SRK_allele_accumulation_analysis.R
  • Execution:
Rscript SRK_allele_accumulation_analysis.R

7.5.6.1 Input files

  • SRK_individual_allele_genotypes.tsv — from Step 11
  • sampling_metadata.csv
  • SRK_individual_BL_assignments.tsv — from Step 13

Filtering criteria:

  • Species level: keep all ingroup individuals (Ingroup == 1) — 272 in the current dataset
  • EO and BL levels: restrict to BL-assigned individuals (BL_status ∈ {Assigned, Inferred}) — 262 in the current dataset
  • Match individuals using SampleID
  • EO-level curves: retain EOs with ≥ 5 individuals; BLs always included regardless of N

7.5.6.2 Output files

  • SRK_allele_accumulation_curves.pdf — species + per-EO + per-BL pages
  • figures/SRK_allele_accumulation_species.png — bar (observed + predicted-undetected) + curve, shared y-axis
  • figures/presentation/SRK_allele_accumulation_species_observed.png / _predicted.png — slide build-up frames
  • figures/SRK_allele_accumulation_combined.png — EO curves colored by parent BL
  • figures/SRK_allele_accumulation_BL_combined.png — BL aggregate curves
  • figures/SRK_allele_accumulation_drift_erosion.png — EO stacked bars sorted by BL with BL color strip
  • figures/SRK_allele_accumulation_BL_drift_erosion.png — BL aggregate stacked bars
  • SRK_allele_accumulation_stats.tsv
  • SRK_species_richness_estimates.tsv

Note: SRK_species_richness_estimates.tsv must be generated before running SRK_chisq_species_population.R, as it provides the empirical species optimum used in the frequency plots.

7.5.6.3 Scientific Applications

Allele accumulation curves provide an empirical framework for testing evolutionary hypotheses in self-incompatibility systems.

Applications include:

7.5.6.4 Detection of Balancing Selection

NFDS maintains many rare alleles, producing accumulation curves with persistent allele discovery.

7.5.6.5 Population Genetic Comparisons

Differences among populations can reveal:

  • bottlenecks
  • founder effects
  • migration patterns

7.5.6.6 Self-Incompatibility Evolution

For SRK loci, accumulation dynamics reflect:

  • maintenance of allelic diversity
  • turnover of functional incompatibility alleles
  • strength of balancing selection across populations.

7.6 Allele Frequency Analysis

7.6.1 Overview

While negative frequency-dependent selection (NFDS) maintains high allelic diversity at the SRK self-incompatibility locus, allele frequencies may still deviate from equal expectations due to demographic processes such as genetic drift, population structure, or recent allele turnover.

This analysis evaluates whether observed SRK allele frequency distributions deviate from the theoretical NFDS expectation, where all alleles should occur at approximately equal frequencies.

A key feature of this pipeline is the use of an empirically estimated species-level allele optimum (from SRK_species_richness_estimates.tsv) rather than an arbitrary fixed value. The Michaelis-Menten asymptote is used by default as it directly reflects the shape of the observed accumulation curve. This optimum sets the x-axis limit for all frequency plots and defines the “missing alleles to optimum” zone, quantifying how far each population is from carrying the full species-level SRK diversity.

The pipeline:

  • Loads the estimated species allele optimum from the accumulation analysis
  • Filters ingroup individuals only
  • Links genotype data to sampling metadata
  • Performs χ² goodness-of-fit tests at the species and population levels
  • Generates allele frequency plots with missing allele zones relative to the estimated optimum
  • Outputs a summary table of statistical tests

7.6.2 Pipeline Steps

7.6.2.1 Species Richness Optimum

At startup the script loads SRK_species_richness_estimates.tsv produced by SRK_allele_accumulation_analysis.R. The Michaelis-Menten estimate is used preferentially as the species optimum; if MM fitting failed, Chao1 is used, then the consensus mean. If the file is absent a fallback value of 50 is applied with a warning.

est_file <- "SRK_species_richness_estimates.tsv"
if (file.exists(est_file)) {
  est_df <- read.table(est_file, header = TRUE, sep = "\t")
  species_optimum <- as.integer(est_df$MM_estimate[1])
}

7.6.2.2 Input Data Integration

Three input datasets are required:

  • SRK species richness estimates (SRK_species_richness_estimates.tsv) — produced by the accumulation analysis
  • SRK protein assignment table containing allele identities per individual
  • Sampling metadata containing population information and ingroup status

Individuals classified as outgroups (Ingroup == 0) are excluded to ensure that only relevant samples are analysed.

Individual identifiers in the genotype dataset are matched with the SampleID column of the metadata table to assign population membership.

7.6.2.3 Allele Frequency Estimation

For each analysis level (species and populations), allele counts are calculated as:

\[ p_i = \frac{n_i}{\sum n_i} \]

where

  • ( n_i ) is the number of observations of allele (i)
  • ( n_i ) is the total number of allele observations

Under ideal NFDS expectations, all alleles should have equal frequency:

\[ p_{expected} = \frac{1}{k} \]

where (k) is the number of alleles present.

7.6.2.4 χ² Goodness-of-Fit Test

To test for deviations from equal allele frequencies, a chi-square goodness-of-fit test is performed:

\[ \chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i} \]

where:

  • (O_i) = observed allele counts
  • (E_i) = expected allele counts assuming equal frequencies

A significant result indicates that allele frequencies are uneven, which may reflect:

  • genetic drift
  • population subdivision
  • sampling effects
  • transient demographic processes

7.6.2.5 Population-Level Filtering

Population-level analyses are restricted to populations with:

  • at least 5 individuals

This filtering avoids unstable statistical estimates caused by very small sample sizes.

7.6.2.6 Allele Frequency Visualization

For each analysis level, the pipeline produces a frequency distribution plot including:

  • Observed allele frequencies (black line, rank-ordered)
  • NFDS expectation (1/k) (blue dashed line)
  • LOESS smoothed trend of the observed distribution (red line)
  • Missing alleles zone (tomato-shaded rectangle) — the gap between the last observed allele and the estimated species optimum, labelled with the count of missing alleles

The x-axis spans from 1 to species_optimum (the MM-estimated total), so all plots share the same scale and deficits are directly comparable across populations.

These visualizations allow rapid inspection of:

  • dominant alleles
  • rare allele tails
  • deviations from theoretical NFDS equilibrium
  • how many alleles each population is missing relative to the species pool.

7.6.3 Output Files

As of 2026-05-07 the script reads SRK_individual_BL_assignments.tsv (Step 13) and tests at three levels with the same split-filter design as Step 15 (species = 335 ingroup; EO/BL = 325 BL-assigned).

7.6.3.1 Statistical Results

SRK_chisq_species_population.tsv

This table summarizes the χ² test statistics at three analysis levels: species, EO (sorted by parent BL), and BL.

Column Description
N_individuals Number of individuals analyzed
N_alleles Number of SRK alleles detected
X2 Chi-square statistic
df Degrees of freedom
p_value Significance level
Level Species, EO, or BL
Population Group identifier (All, EO27, BL2, …)
BL Parent BL for EO rows; the BL itself for BL rows; NA for the species row

Example output (current dataset, 2026-05-07):

N_individuals N_alleles X2 df p_value Level Population BL
272 54 2438.84 53 ~0 Species All NA
48 6 113.65 5 6.9e-23 EO EO70 BL2
62 13 151.33 12 3.0e-26 EO EO76 BL3
42 22 111.95 21 2.1e-14 EO EO27 BL4
53 14 60.68 13 4.0e-08 EO EO25 BL5
5 4 4.64 3 0.20 BL BL1 BL1
51 8 174.70 7 2.6e-34 BL BL2 BL2
78 31 324.66 30 3.5e-51 BL BL4 BL4

All medium and large groups (N ≥ 36) reject the uniform-frequency null at p < 1e-7 — confirming that drift has skewed allele frequencies away from the NFDS equilibrium at every analysis level. Only EO18 and BL1 (both N = 5) fail to reach significance, which is a power limitation rather than a finding of evenness.

7.6.3.2 Frequency Plots

SRK_chisq_species_population_frequency_plots.pdf

12 pages: 1 species + 6 EOs (sorted by parent BL) + 5 BLs. Each EO and BL page is colored by its parent BL using the locked Set1 palette (Step 14), so the BL identity is visually obvious without reading the legend. The species page uses neutral grey.

7.6.4 Biological Interpretation

Results can be interpreted using the following guidelines:

7.6.4.1 Non-significant deviation

If the chi-square test is not significant:

A chi-square test revealed no significant deviation from equal allele frequencies ((^2 = X), df = (Y), (p = Z)), consistent with negative frequency-dependent selection maintaining SRK allele diversity.

7.6.4.2 Significant deviation

If the chi-square test is significant:

Allele frequencies deviated significantly from equal expectations ((^2 = X), df = (Y), (p = Z)), suggesting that additional evolutionary or demographic processes may influence SRK allele frequencies.

Possible explanations include:

  • population bottlenecks
  • local drift
  • unequal migration rates
  • recent allele turnover

7.6.5 Bioinformatics

  • Script: SRK_chisq_species_population.R
  • Run command:
Rscript SRK_chisq_species_population.R

7.6.5.1 Input Files

  • SRK_species_richness_estimates.tsv — produced by SRK_allele_accumulation_analysis.R (run first)
  • SRK_individual_allele_genotypes.tsv — allele count matrix (individuals × allele bins, values = copy counts)
  • sampling_metadata.csv

7.6.5.2 Output Files

  • SRK_chisq_species_population.tsv
  • SRK_chisq_species_population_frequency_plots.pdf

7.6.5.3 R Script

############################################################
# SRK allele frequency χ² tests
# Uses allele count matrix for proper copy-number frequencies
############################################################

cat("\nStarting SRK chi-square analysis\n")

############################################################
# 1. Load files
############################################################

geno <- read.table(
  "SRK_individual_allele_genotypes.tsv",
  header = TRUE,
  sep = "\t",
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE,
  check.names = FALSE
)

meta_df <- read.csv(
  "sampling_metadata.csv",
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

############################################################
# 2. Keep ingroup only
############################################################

meta_df <- meta_df[meta_df$Ingroup == 1, ]

geno <- geno[geno$Individual %in% meta_df$SampleID, ]

geno$Population <- meta_df$Pop[match(geno$Individual, meta_df$SampleID)]

allele_cols <- setdiff(colnames(geno), c("Individual", "Population"))

geno[, allele_cols] <- lapply(geno[, allele_cols], as.numeric)

cat("Individuals analysed:", nrow(geno), "\n")
cat("Allele bins:", length(allele_cols), "\n")

7.7 TP1 — Mating-pool Functionality

Requires the allele count matrix (Step 11), the inferred tetraploid zygosity table (Step 12), the BL assignments (Step 13), and the sampling metadata. The pre-existing erosion barplots from Steps 15–16 already document depletion against the species optimum; TP1 takes that as given and asks a different question.

Scripts:

  1. SRK_TP1_compatibility_metrics.py — computes per-EO and per-BL metrics (including bootstrap 95 % CIs on P_compat and the Depletion Index DI = 1 − k / k_species), writes Tables/SRK_EO_allele_richness.tsv
  2. SRK_P_compat_traffic_light.R — stakeholder-facing red/amber/green priority figure (full + blank variants); the dedicated random-mating-viability diagnostic foregrounded in the conservation report and presentations
  3. SRK_depletion_ranking.R — conservation-ranking figure placing each group on P_compat × DI axes with quadrants labelled by the three breeding strategies in Tables/SRK_breeding_strategies.csv (HEALTHY / INFORMED BREEDING (frequency skew) / INFORMED BREEDING + ALLELE INJECTION (preventive / urgent)). Two panels (_observed uses sample-size-corrected k; _predicted uses the MM asymptote) × three variants (_blank zones only, _EOs focal EOs only, _all BLs + EOs) = 6 figures for layered presentation builds

Command:

python3 SRK_TP1_compatibility_metrics.py
Rscript SRK_P_compat_traffic_light.R
Rscript SRK_depletion_ranking.R

Inputs: - Tables/SRK_individual_allele_genotypes.tsv — Step 11 count matrix - Tables/sampling_metadata.csv — per-individual EO assignment - SRK_individual_BL_assignments.tsv — Step 13 BL labels - Tables/SRK_individual_zygosity.tsv — Step 12 inferred tetraploid genotype classes (the metrics script applies the same inference rule directly from the raw matrix; the zygosity TSV is the validated reference)

7.7.1 What TP1 measures — is the depleted population still functioning?

TP1 was reframed on 2026-05-22. The previous formulation (prop_optimum × Ne/N_alleles) repeated, in a scatter, what the Step 15 erosion barplots already show: how depleted each population is relative to the species-level optimum (≈ 58 alleles). The reframed TP1 asks the complementary question:

Given that this population is depleted, is its mating pool still functioning, and what conservation intervention does the data support?

Two axes diagnose mating-pool function:

  • x = evenness J (Shannon H / ln k) — how evenly are the surviving alleles held? J = 1 at NFDS equilibrium; J → 0 as one allele dominates (drift signature).
  • y = P_compat — the compatible-pair fraction — what fraction of random plant pairs are mating-compatible under tetraploid sporophytic SI? A value of 0.40 means roughly 40 % of random pairs in the population can produce seed. Computed exactly from the inferred allele frequencies (formula below) as a deterministic closed-form value; uncertainty is quantified by a bootstrap 95 % CI (1000 resamples of individuals with replacement) reported alongside each point estimate. Directly demographic — translates to expected fraction of compatible crosses.

Point size encodes rarefied k (richness at N = 30, 1000 perms) so depletion remains visible without dominating the figure.

7.7.2 How tetraploidy is handled

LEPA is tetraploid (2n = 4x). Amplicon SRK sequencing recovers ≤ 4 allele copies per individual but often returns fewer because of allele drop-out. Step 12 (SRK_zygosity_from_genotype.R) infers the full tetraploid genotype with this rule:

Distinct alleles observed Inferred tetraploid genotype Logic
1 AAAA fill to 4 copies of A
2, dominant ≥ 2× minor AAAB 3 + 1
2, roughly equal AABB 2 + 2
3 AABC 2 + 1 + 1
4 ABCD 1 + 1 + 1 + 1

The metrics script applies this same rule before counting allele copies, so every individual contributes exactly 4 inferred copies. The per-EO denominator for allele frequencies is C = 4N.

7.7.3 Compatibility formula

Sporophytic SI, tetraploid stigma (up to 4 distinct alleles), diploid pollen (up to 2 distinct alleles), co-dominance. Pollen is rejected if any of its 2 alleles matches any of the stigma’s 4. The exact strict-SI formula under multinomial allele sampling is

\[ P_\text{compat strict} \;=\; \sum_{a, b} p_a\, p_b\, \bigl(1 - p_a - p_b \cdot \mathbb{I}[a \ne b]\bigr)^4. \]

Equal-frequency benchmark: \(1 - 8/k\) (much harsher than the diploid \(1 - 2/k\) — tetraploid SI requires roughly four times the allelic richness to reach the same compatibility level).

7.7.4 SI leakage and the C3 Stage 5 connection

At leakage rate L ∈ [0, 1], a fraction L of would-be-incompatible matings succeed anyway (selfing or SI-leaky outcrossing):

\[ P_\text{compat}(L) \;=\; P_\text{compat strict} + L \cdot \bigl(1 - P_\text{compat strict}\bigr). \]

The script reports P_compat at L ∈ {0, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50}. An empirical estimate L̂ = prop_AAAA / 3.5 is also computed per group: AAAA cannot arise under strict outcrossing SI, so observed AAAA reflects historical selfing or sib-crossing. The 3.5 divisor is a population-averaged tetrasomic correction (AABB self → AAAA in ~1/6 of offspring; AAAB self → AAAA in ~1/2; averaged over the AABB/AAAB mix typical of the data, the effective correction is ~3–4). L̂ is an upper bound because some apparent AAAA arises from amplicon under-recovery.

7.7.5 Inheritance-mode caveat

The exact P_compat formula assumes tetrasomic inheritance (random pairing across all four chromosomes — autotetraploid-like). If LEPA’s SRK locus shows disomic inheritance (two homoeologous subgenomes segregating independently), the system behaves more like two superimposed diploid SI systems, which is generally more permissive — current P_compat is then a conservative lower bound. The formula should be revisited if cytology or segregation data establish disomic inheritance at SRK.

7.7.6 Quadrants map directly to conservation actions

Region Reading Intervention
top-right (high J, high P_compat) functioning, even — NFDS still works MONITOR + augment for long-term sustainability
top-left (low J, high P_compat) mating works despite frequency skew AUGMENT to restore evenness
bottom-right (high J, low P_compat) evenly held but too few alleles AUGMENT URGENTLY (too few compatible mates)
bottom-left (low J, low P_compat) skewed and depleted — mating pool collapsed BIOBANK + RESTORE

TP1 thresholds (adjustable at top of R script):

Parameter Default Criterion
TP1_J 0.80 evenness floor (frequencies skewed from NFDS expectation)
TP1_P_COMPAT 0.40 mating-pool floor (fewer than 40% of random pairs compatible)

Per-EO inclusion threshold is N ≥ 15 individuals (smaller EOs lack power for k_rarefied). All five BL aggregates are always plotted.

7.7.7 Random-mating viability — traffic-light figure

  • figures/SRK_P_compat_traffic_light_EO.{png,pdf} — stakeholder-facing red/amber/green priority figure. Six focal EOs ranked worst-to-best by strict-SI P_compat, with bootstrap 95 % CIs as horizontal error bars and BL colour squares on the left edge. The dedicated answer to “is random mating still viable?” — used in the LEPA report and in conservation presentations.
  • figures/SRK_P_compat_traffic_light_EO_blank.{png,pdf} — companion blank variant (zones + EO labels + BL strip + legend; no data drawn) for the predictions slide.

7.7.8 Depletion Index and the conservation-ranking figure

SRK_depletion_ranking.R produces a second 2D figure that aligns directly with the conservation decision the team has to make. Axes:

  • x = Depletion Index (DI) = 1 − k / k_species, where k_species = 59 (Step 15 MM consensus). DI = 0 means at species equilibrium; DI = 1 means no alleles retained. Two flavours are written to the metrics TSV:
    • DI_observed uses k_rarefied30 (sample-size-corrected), so EOs / BLs with different N are directly comparable. The conservative read.
    • DI_predicted uses the per-group MM asymptote k_predicted_MM from Step 15. It represents the upper bound on richness achievable from existing sampling without further intervention — gaps between observed and predicted DI flag populations where additional sampling would likely recover more alleles.
  • y = compatible-pair fraction under strict tetraploid SI (P_compat_L0), with the bootstrap 95 % CI drawn as a vertical whisker.

Quadrants are labelled with the three breeding strategies defined in Tables/SRK_breeding_strategies.csv:

DI P_compat Strategy
< 0.50 ≥ 0.40 HEALTHY — random pollination
≥ 0.50 ≥ 0.40 INFORMED BREEDING + ALLELE INJECTION (preventive)
< 0.50 < 0.40 INFORMED BREEDING (frequency skew)
≥ 0.50 < 0.40 INFORMED BREEDING + ALLELE INJECTION (urgent)

Two panels are rendered (_observed and _predicted), each in three layered variants for stakeholder presentations:

  • figures/SRK_depletion_ranking_{observed,predicted}_blank.{png,pdf} — zones + labels only (predictions slide)
  • figures/SRK_depletion_ranking_{observed,predicted}_EOs.{png,pdf} — focal EOs only (mid-build; EOs are the management units)
  • figures/SRK_depletion_ranking_{observed,predicted}_all.{png,pdf} — BLs + EOs together (final reveal)

In the current dataset every group except EO27 falls in one of the two ALLELE INJECTION quadrants. EO27 sits in the INFORMED BREEDING (frequency skew) quadrant on the _predicted panel and at the threshold on _observed. The observed-vs-predicted gap is largest for EO27 and BL4 (substantial sampling-driven contribution to apparent depletion) and smallest for EO70 (near-asymptote — depletion is real).

7.7.9 Outputs

  • Tables/SRK_EO_allele_richness.tsv — 5 BL rows + 6 EO rows × 31 columns: identifiers (level, group, BL, N), depletion (k_species, k_predicted_MM, DI_observed, DI_predicted), genotype mix (geno_mix, prop_AAAA, L_hat_from_AAAA, raw_copy_recovery), richness (k_observed, k_rarefied30_mean/sd), evenness (shannon_H, evenness_J, chi2_uniform_p, P_compat_uniform_4x), and the leakage ladder (P_compat_L0/L0.10/L0.25/L0.50 each with _lo/_hi bootstrap CIs)
  • Tables/SRK_breeding_strategies.csv — three-row reference table (Random pollination / Informed breeding / Informed breeding + allele injection) describing how parents are paired and what each strategy produces; provides the vocabulary used in the depletion-ranking quadrants
  • Six PNG + six PDF depletion-ranking panels (observed/predicted × blank/EOs/all)
  • One PNG + one PDF traffic-light figure (SRK_P_compat_traffic_light_EO) + matching _blank variant

7.7.10 Headline result (current dataset)

Traffic-light: EO70 is the only RED population (P_compat ≈ 0.08, well below the 0.20 failure threshold); EO67 / EO18 / EO76 are AMBER; EO25 / EO27 sit at the GREEN border. Depletion ranking: every group except EO27 falls in one of the two ALLELE INJECTION quadrants. EO27 sits in the INFORMED BREEDING (frequency skew) quadrant on the _predicted panel and at the threshold on _observed; the observed-vs-predicted gap is largest for EO27 and BL4 (further sampling would likely recover more alleles) and smallest for EO70 (near-asymptote — depletion is real).

Legacy: the prior implementation SRK_TP1_tipping_point.R is archived under archive/.

7.8 Allele Composition Comparison

7.8.1 Overview

This step quantifies how S-allele sets are partitioned and shared across Element Occurrences (EOs). While allele accumulation curves (Step 15) and frequency analyses (Step 16) characterise diversity within and relative to the species optimum, this analysis asks a complementary question: which alleles are exclusive to a single EO, which are shared between subsets, and which are ubiquitous? The answer directly informs cross-population breeding decisions — EOs that share few alleles are the most important exchange partners for increasing allele richness in the focal population.

Two complementary visualisations are produced:

  1. UpSet plot — displays all 2ⁿ−1 non-empty intersection sizes for n EOs, sorted by allele count. Single-EO bars (private alleles) are colour-coded per EO; multi-EO intersections are shown in grey with dot-matrix membership indicators below.
  2. Pairwise sharing heatmap — a symmetric n × n matrix showing the raw count of alleles present in both EOs of each pair. Diagonal values equal each EO’s total allele richness.

7.8.2 Scientific Rationale

Under negative frequency-dependent selection (NFDS), S-alleles are maintained at approximate frequency equality and are expected to be widely distributed across populations over evolutionary time. Conversely, genetic drift and habitat fragmentation lead to allele loss and population-private allele compositions — each remnant population retains only a subset of the ancestral species repertoire, with little overlap between populations. The UpSet and heatmap visualisations make this partitioning explicit:

  • Many private alleles per EO: indicates strong historical isolation and drift; each EO carries unique diversity that cannot be recovered from any other.
  • Very few alleles shared across all EOs: confirms that the species-wide allele pool is distributed rather than redundant — any single EO is an inadequate proxy for species diversity.
  • Low pairwise sharing: identifies which EO pairs are most compositionally distinct, making them the highest-priority partners for managed cross-population transfers.

2026-05-07 update: the script now reads SRK_individual_BL_assignments.tsv (Step 13) instead of raw Pop from sampling metadata, and produces UpSet + heatmap plots at TWO levels in parallel: EO (sorted by parent BL, dots colored by BL) and BL (5 lineages, colored by BL).

7.8.3 Bioinformatics

  • Scripts:
    • SRK_allele_sharing_EOs.py — UpSet plots + pairwise heatmaps (EO + BL)
    • SRK_allele_eulerr_BLs.R — area-proportional 5-set Euler diagram of BL allele sets (stakeholder-facing companion to the BL UpSet; requires the R eulerr package)
  • Environments: polyploid-model (Python: pandas, numpy, matplotlib) and base R + eulerr
  • Execution (both scripts; regenerate together as the dataset grows):
python3 SRK_allele_sharing_EOs.py
Rscript SRK_allele_eulerr_BLs.R

7.8.3.1 Key Parameters

Parameter Default Description
--genotypes SRK_individual_allele_genotypes.tsv Allele count matrix from Step 11
--bl-assignments SRK_individual_BL_assignments.tsv Bridge table from Step 13
--min-samples 5 Minimum individuals per EO; BLs always included
--pdf-dir . Output directory for PDFs
--png-dir figures Output directory for PNGs

7.8.3.2 Input Files

  • SRK_individual_allele_genotypes.tsv — allele count matrix from Step 11
  • SRK_individual_BL_assignments.tsv — per-individual EO/BL labels from Step 13 (filtered to BL_status ∈ Assigned ∪ Inferred)

7.8.3.3 Output Files (PDFs at root, PNGs in figures/)

  • SRK_allele_upset_EOs.{pdf,png} — EO UpSet, dots colored by parent BL
  • SRK_allele_sharing_heatmap_EOs.{pdf,png} — pairwise EO sharing heatmap
  • SRK_allele_upset_BLs.{pdf,png} — NEW, BL-level UpSet
  • SRK_allele_sharing_heatmap_BLs.{pdf,png} — NEW, 5×5 BL pairwise heatmap
  • figures/SRK_allele_eulerr_BLs.{png,pdf} — NEW (2026-05-22), area-proportional 5-set Euler diagram of BL allele sets. Same data as the BL UpSet but rendered as familiar Venn-style ellipses for stakeholder presentations. Produced by the R companion script (SRK_allele_eulerr_BLs.R).

7.8.3.4 Headline Result (Current Dataset)

The BL-level UpSet is the direct test of independent bottlenecks. Out of 54 observed alleles:

BL Total alleles Private alleles
BL1 4 0
BL2 8 2
BL3 16 11
BL4 31 14
BL5 18 6

26 of 49 alleles (53 %) are private to a single BL (current dataset, 2026-05-11); only 2 alleles (Allele_050 and Allele_051) are shared across all 5 BLs. The BL4 ↔︎ BL5 pair shares 7 alleles (highest off-diagonal in the heatmap), hinting at limited historical contact between these two lineages.

7.8.3.5 Scientific Applications

  • Identify alleles unique to one BL — these are evolutionary-scale irreplaceable; targeted inter-BL crosses are the only way to recover them in other lineages
  • Quantify lineage-private vs. shared diversity to test whether bottlenecks have been independent or shared
  • Guide inter-BL transfer priorities: BL pairs with the lowest pairwise sharing offer the greatest complementarity for restoring species-level allele richness

7.9 Individual Genotypic Fitness Score

7.9.1 Overview

This analysis introduces the Genotypic Fitness Score (GFS) — a per-individual metric that quantifies reproductive potential under the tetraploid SI system. GFS is defined as the proportion of diploid gametes that carry two distinct alleles (heterozygous gametes), computed directly from allele copy numbers at the SRK locus.

The key motivation is that two individuals carrying the same number of unique alleles can have very different seed production potential depending on the dosage balance of those alleles. For example:

  • AABB (two alleles, 2 copies each): produces 4 of 6 possible gamete types as heterozygous → GFS = 0.667
  • AAAB (two alleles, 3+1 copies): produces only 3 of 6 gamete types as heterozygous → GFS = 0.500

This distinction is invisible to simple zygosity classification (both are “2-allele heterozygotes”) but has direct implications for managed crossing and seed orchard design.

7.9.2 Biological Rationale

In a tetraploid, gametes are formed by sampling 2 alleles from the 4 copies at the SRK locus (C(4,2) = 6 equally likely combinations). The GFS captures the fraction of these gametes that are heterozygous — i.e., carry two different alleles. Heterozygous gametes maximise:

  1. Compatibility range: offspring receiving two distinct alleles are less likely to be incompatible with any given mate
  2. Allele diversity contribution: heterozygous gametes pass both alleles to the next generation, preventing allele loss through dosage asymmetry
  3. NFDS benefit: diverse offspring have higher reproductive success under negative frequency-dependent selection

7.9.3 Formula

\[\text{GFS}_i = 1 - \frac{\displaystyle\sum_k n_k\,(n_k - 1)}{12}\]

where \(n_k\) is the copy number of allele \(k\) in individual \(i\), summed over all alleles present, and \(12 = 2 \times \binom{4}{2}\) normalises to the full gamete space.

7.9.4 Genotype Class Mapping

Genotype Copy-number profile GFS Heterozygous gametes
ABCD 1, 1, 1, 1 1.000 6 / 6
AABC 2, 1, 1 0.833 5 / 6
AABB 2, 2 0.667 4 / 6
AAAB 3, 1 0.500 3 / 6
AAAA 4 0.000 0 / 6

7.9.5 Bioinformatics

  • Script: SRK_individual_GFS.R
  • Environment: base R + tidyverse + ggplot2; ggrepel optional (for labelled scatter plot)
  • Execution (run from project root):
Rscript SRK_individual_GFS.R

7.9.5.1 Input Files

File From step Description
SRK_individual_zygosity.tsv Step 12 Per-individual tetraploid genotype pattern (AAAA/AAAB/AABB/AABC/ABCD)
SRK_individual_BL_assignments.tsv Step 13 EO and BL assignments (325 BL-assigned of 335 ingroup individuals)

BL-stratified design. EO and BL summaries are computed in parallel using the Step 13 bridge. EOs are sorted by parent BL throughout (Set1 palette, matches LEPA_EO_spatial_clustering); BL aggregates pool all BL-assigned individuals.

7.9.5.2 Key Parameters (top of script)

Parameter Default Description
EO_MIN_N 5 Minimum sample size for an EO to appear in plots; BLs always plotted
BL_PALETTE Set1 (from srk_bl_constants.R) BL1=#984EA3, BL2=#377EB8, BL3=#E41A1C, BL4=#FF7F00, BL5=#4DAF4A
TP2_MEAN_GFS 0.667 Mean GFS threshold for TP2 breach
TP2_PROP_AAAA 0.30 Proportion AAAA threshold for TP2 breach

7.9.5.3 Output Files

File Content
SRK_individual_GFS.tsv Per-individual GFS, genotype class, EO, BL, Drift_index, method

7.9.5.4 Diagnostic Plots

  1. Proportional stacked bar — genotype class composition per EO (sorted by parent BL, BL-coloured x-axis labels), with 30% AAAA threshold marked
  2. Individual GFS jitter + mean crossbar — per-individual scores with AABB (0.667) and AAAB (0.500) threshold lines

7.10 TP2 Tipping Point Analysis

Requires SRK_individual_GFS.tsv from Step 19. Run after Step 19 has completed.

7.10.1 Overview

The TP2 Tipping Point Analysis assesses reproductive fitness at the population level — the degree to which individuals within an EO can actually participate in compatible crosses and contribute allelic diversity to the next generation. While Step 19 characterises each individual’s gametic diversity potential, this step synthesises those scores at the EO level and combines them with AAAA prevalence into a single diagnostic framework. Two complementary questions define the two axes:

  • What fraction of individuals are reproductive dead-ends? (x-axis: prop_AAAA) — An AAAA individual cannot contribute to any compatible cross regardless of its mate, making it a complete loss to the breeding pool. As this fraction grows, the effective breeding population shrinks accordingly.
  • How much reproductive capacity does the average individual still have? (y-axis: mean GFS) — Even individuals that are not dead-ends vary in how much allelic diversity they can contribute per cross. When the population mean falls below the AABB benchmark (GFS = 0.667), even the functional breeders are producing predominantly homotypic gametes.

Together, the two axes establish whether reproductive fitness has degraded beyond the point where within-EO crossing alone can recover it.

7.10.2 Tipping Point 2 — Thresholds

Two threshold conditions are evaluated simultaneously for each EO and each BL:

Threshold Criterion Biological meaning
TP2-AAAA prop(AAAA) > 0.30 More than 30% of individuals are reproductive dead-ends
TP2-mean mean GFS < 0.667 The average individual has less reproductive capacity than an AABB genotype

A group (EO or BL) is flagged CRITICAL when both thresholds are breached simultaneously; AT RISK when either one is breached; OK otherwise.

7.10.3 Bioinformatics

The TP2 analysis is performed by the same script as Step 19 (SRK_individual_GFS.R). EO-level and BL-level summaries are computed in parallel; the TP2 figure plots EOs as circles and BLs as triangles on the same axes, both coloured by parent BL using the locked Set1 palette.

Headline. All 5 BLs are CRITICAL on TP2 (BL3 worst at mean GFS = 0.196, prop AAAA = 0.662); 5 of 6 plotted EOs (N ≥ 5) are CRITICAL — only EO67 is AT RISK rather than CRITICAL.

7.10.3.1 Output Files

File Content
SRK_EO_GFS_summary.tsv EO-level mean GFS, class proportions, TP2 status flags, BL column
SRK_BL_GFS_summary.tsv NEW — BL-level mean GFS, class proportions, TP2 status flags
SRK_GFS_plots.pdf Multi-page PDF: composition (proportional + counts), jitter, TP2 scatter — EOs sorted by parent BL with BL-coloured x-axis labels
figures/SRK_GFS_plots_p3_TP2_tipping_point.png TP2 scatter — EOs (circles) + BLs (triangles), both Set1-coloured
figures/SRK_GFS_plots_p3_TP2_tipping_point_blank.png Same plot without data points, for presentation use

7.10.3.2 Diagnostic Plots

  1. TP2 tipping point map — scatter plot of prop(AAAA) × mean GFS with colour-coded zone polygons for OK, AT RISK, and CRITICAL categories; EOs as circles, BLs as triangles, all Set1-coloured by parent BL
  2. Absolute count stacked bar — raw individual counts per genotype class per EO (sorted by parent BL)

7.10.4 Scientific Applications

  • EO prioritisation: EOs in the CRITICAL quadrant (high prop_AAAA AND low mean_GFS) need both allele importation (TP1 intervention) and targeted within-EO crossing to rebuild AABB/AABC/ABCD individuals
  • Seed parent selection: rank individuals within each EO by GFS; AABC and AABB individuals are priority seed parents over AAAB and AAAA
  • Crossing design: pairing two AAAB individuals carrying different allele pairs can produce AABB or AABC offspring — GFS identifies which pairs are worth prioritising
  • Monitoring: GFS can be tracked across generations to evaluate whether managed crossing is effectively redistributing allele dosage toward AABB/AABC/ABCD classes

7.11 Reproductive Effort Support per Element Occurrence and Bottleneck Lineage

Requires SRK_individual_GFS.tsv from Step 19 (now carries the BL column from the Step 13 bridge).

7.11.1 Overview

This analysis visualises the proportion of individuals at each GFS tier — first per Element Occurrence (EO), then per Bottleneck Lineage (BL) — making explicit the fraction of each group that actively contributes allelic diversity to compatible crosses. The figure translates the GFS principle into a population-level diagnostic: for each EO and each BL, it shows what fraction of individuals can “support reproductive effort” (GFS > 0 — carrying at least two distinct alleles and producing at least one heterozygous gamete combination) and how those individuals are distributed across GFS tiers.

7.11.2 Biological Rationale

AAAA individuals (GFS = 0) carry a single SRK allele across all four genome copies. All six of their diploid gamete combinations are homozygous for that allele, meaning they contribute no allelic diversity to crosses and cannot drive compatible pairings with any partner carrying the same allele. All other genotype classes (AAAB through ABCD) carry at least two distinct alleles and produce at least 3 of 6 heterozygous gametes — they “support reproductive effort” in the sense that their gametes diversify the allele combinations available to offspring.

The proportion of supporting individuals per EO and per BL, combined with the GFS tier distribution within that fraction, provides a direct measure of how much reproductive capacity each unit retains. The BL panel adds a test of the independent-bottleneck hypothesis: if drift trajectories were independent, the AAAA-allele identities should differ across BLs.

7.11.3 Bioinformatics

  • Script: SRK_GFS_reproductive_effort.R
  • Environment: base R + tidyverse + ggplot2
  • Execution (run from project root):
Rscript SRK_GFS_reproductive_effort.R

7.11.3.1 Input Files

File From step Description
SRK_individual_GFS.tsv Step 19 Per-individual GFS scores, genotype classes, EO + BL + Drift_index
SRK_individual_zygosity.tsv Step 12 Per-individual allele composition used to identify the single allele carried by AAAA individuals

BL-stratified design. EOs are ordered by parent BL (total habitat area, then within-BL connectivity, largest at top) then by ascending mean Drift_index within BL; y-axis labels are coloured by parent BL (Set1 palette). The BL panel pools all BL-assigned individuals into 5 lineage rows, ordered by total habitat area then within-BL connectivity (BL4, BL5, BL3, BL1, BL2 — largest at top). The TP2 AAAA threshold (30%) is marked on both panels.

Pan-group AAAA alleles. Pan-BL alleles are present in AAAA individuals across every BL; pan-EO alleles are present in AAAA individuals in ≥80% of focus EOs (relaxed threshold accommodates EO18, the smallest focus EO with only 4 AAAA individuals carrying a different drift signature). Allele_050 and Allele_051 (Synonymy group 1, HV-identical, likely the same SI specificity) appear as pan-BL across all 5 BLs and pan-EO in 5 of 6 focus EOs — confirming that independent bottlenecks have all converged on the same fixed SI specificity.

7.11.3.2 Key Parameters (top of script)

Parameter Default Description
EO_MIN_N 5 Minimum sample size for an EO to appear in plots (matches Steps 17/19)
BL_PALETTE Set1 (from srk_bl_constants.R) BL1=#984EA3, BL2=#377EB8, BL3=#E41A1C, BL4=#FF7F00, BL5=#4DAF4A
TP2_PROP_AAAA 0.30 Threshold line position; matches TP2 threshold from Step 20
PAN_EO_FRAC 0.80 Allele must appear in AAAA individuals in ≥80% of focus EOs to be flagged pan-EO

7.11.3.3 Output Files

File Content
SRK_GFS_reproductive_effort.pdf 2-page PDF: EO panel + BL panel — GFS tier composition with reproductive effort annotations
SRK_GFS_AAAA_allele_composition.pdf 2-page PDF: EO panel + BL panel — allele identity of AAAA individuals; Synonymy group 1 alleles highlighted
SRK_BL_reproductive_effort_summary.tsv NEW — BL-level reproductive effort summary (n, n_supporting, prop_supporting, prop_AAAA, mean_GFS)
figures/SRK_GFS_reproductive_effort_EO.png EO-level proportional bar (PNG)
figures/SRK_GFS_reproductive_effort_BL.png NEW — BL-level proportional bar (PNG)
figures/SRK_GFS_AAAA_allele_composition_EO.png EO-level AAAA allele identity (PNG)
figures/SRK_GFS_AAAA_allele_composition_BL.png NEW — BL-level AAAA allele identity (PNG)

7.11.3.4 Figure Descriptions

SRK_GFS_reproductive_effort (page 1, EO panel): Each horizontal bar represents one EO with N ≥ 5. EOs are ordered by parent BL (total habitat area, then within-BL connectivity, largest at top) then by ascending mean Drift_index within BL; y-axis labels are coloured by parent BL using the Set1 palette. Segments are coloured by GFS class (red = AAAA through blue = ABCD), stacked left to right from lowest to highest GFS. The dashed vertical line marks the TP2 AAAA threshold (30%): bars where the red (AAAA) segment extends past this line indicate a TP2 breach. Annotations to the right show proportion supporting (%), count (n / N), and mean GFS.

SRK_GFS_reproductive_effort (page 2, BL panel): Same layout for the 5 BLs, ordered by total habitat area then within-BL connectivity (BL4, BL5, BL3, BL1, BL2 — largest at top). All 325 BL-assigned individuals are aggregated into their respective BL rows; y-axis labels are coloured by BL.

SRK_GFS_AAAA_allele_composition (page 1, EO panel): Companion figure unpacking the AAAA bar from the EO reproductive effort figure. Each bar shows how many AAAA individuals in that EO carry each allele. Pan-EO Synonymy group 1 alleles (Allele_050, Allele_051; orange and blue) are highlighted; all remaining alleles are shown as a single grey “Other alleles” segment. Annotations show total AAAA count and percentage carrying a Synonymy group 1 allele.

SRK_GFS_AAAA_allele_composition (page 2, BL panel): Same composition shown across the 5 BLs. Allele_050 and Allele_051 are pan-BL (5/5 BLs) — confirms shared Synonymy group 1 fixation despite independent bottlenecks. BL2 reaches 69% Synonymy group 1 saturation; BL5 is the lowest at 24%.

7.11.4 Scientific Applications

  • Conservation triage: per-EO and per-BL proportions of supporting individuals quantify how much of each unit is functional for managed crossing.
  • Seed parent pool size: the annotated n / N count identifies candidate individuals for inclusion in a crossing programme.
  • Cross-EO comparison within BL: BL-grouped EO ordering highlights drift heterogeneity within each BL — e.g., EO67 (47% support, BL4) is much less degraded than EO27 (36%, same BL).
  • Independent-bottleneck test: pan-BL Allele_050/057 confirms that drift across all five lineages has settled on the same Synonymy group 1 specificity; the only EO without Synonymy group 1 in its AAAA pool (EO18) is also the smallest, suggesting sample size rather than a divergent drift trajectory.

  • Communication: both figures are designed for presentation to conservation managers, translating GFS and allele identity into intuitive breeding-pool diagnostics.

8 Phase 5: Testing S-allele Hypotheses

8.1 Scientific Approach

Phase 5 translates the sequence-based allele bins defined in Phase 2 into testable hypotheses about recognition specificity, using controlled crosses between genotyped individuals to validate whether sequence similarity predicts functional SI identity.

The central question is: do alleles that are closely related in sequence share the same recognition specificity, or are they functionally distinct? This matters because the allele bins produced in Step 10 are defined by sequence distance alone — two alleles grouped into the same bin are hypothesised to be functionally equivalent (same “lock”), but this has not been confirmed experimentally. Conversely, allele bins that are closely related but fall just outside the clustering threshold might still share the same recognition specificity and could be treated as synonymous for crossing purposes.

The experimental logic rests on four cross categories:

  • W — HV-identical (same allele bin, HV distance = 0): pollen and pistil carry the same SRK allele at all hypervariable positions. Under a functional SI system, these crosses should fail — the pistil recognises the pollen as self and rejects it. These are negative controls. Any seed set observed in Incompatible crosses is direct evidence of SI breakdown, consistent with Stage 5 of the Compatibility Collapse Cascade.

  • N — near-synonymy test (different allele bins, HV distance > 0 and < 0.04, same phylogenetic class): pollen and pistil carry alleles that differ at a small number of hypervariable positions. These crosses are the core of the hypothesis test — seed failure would indicate the allele bins are functionally synonymous (merge them); seed set would confirm the small HV difference is functionally real (keep bins separate).

  • Compatible_within — within-class positive control (HV distance ≥ 0.04, same phylogenetic class): pollen and pistil carry alleles that are substantially divergent within the same class. These crosses are expected to succeed and serve as within-class positive controls.

  • Compatible_cross — between-class control (different phylogenetic classes): pollen and pistil carry alleles from Class I and Class II respectively. These crosses are guaranteed to succeed — between-class compatibility is maximally divergent. They anchor the upper end of the seed yield scale.

Why AAAA individuals? Individuals homozygous for a single allele across all four genome copies (AAAA genotype; 60% of all sampled plants, n = 164) are the ideal cross parents for this experiment. Their pollen carries a single SCR identity — the allele assignment is unambiguous, and cross outcomes can be interpreted without accounting for pollen genotype distributions. Crosses between two AAAA plants are therefore the cleanest test of allele-level recognition specificity available in a tetraploid system.

Two-step analysis: Step 26 is itself split into two scripts. Step 26a (srk_variability_landscape.py) builds a multi-species alignment of LEPA + Brassica rapa/B. oleracea + Arabidopsis lyrata/A. halleri SRK alleles, applies an identical sliding-window Shannon-entropy scan to each species, and tests whether LEPA HV regions co-locate with Brassica/Arabidopsis HV regions more than expected by chance (permutation test). The validated LEPA HV columns are written to SRK_LEPA_HV_positions.tsv. Step 26b (srk_allele_hypotheses.py) reads those canonical HV columns, computes HV-only pairwise distances, detects phylogenetic classes by UPGMA, generates the prioritised crossing plan, and constructs the synonymy network. Step 27 reads completed cross records and tests whether the Incompatible / Synonymy_test / Compatible_within / Compatible_cross category predicts seed yield.

8.2 Hypervariable-Position Allele Hypothesis Testing and Crossing Design

Requires SRK_protein_allele_representatives.fasta from Step 10, SRK_individual_zygosity.tsv from Step 12, and SRK_individual_metadata.tsv from Step 11. Run after Steps 10, 11, and 12 have completed.

8.2.1 Overview

Step 26 is split into five scripts (26a–26e):

  • Step 26a — Cross-Brassicaceae Variability Landscape (srk_variability_landscape.py): builds a combined alignment of the LEPA representative SRK alleles + 22 Brassica rapa/B. oleracea SRK references + 10 Arabidopsis lyrata/A. halleri SRK references, applies an identical sliding-window Shannon-entropy scan to each species, calls per-species HV regions at mean + 1×SD of the smoothed profile, and runs a 10 000-permutation test of pairwise HV-region overlap (LEPA↔︎Brassica, LEPA↔︎Arabidopsis, Brassica↔︎Arabidopsis). The 12 SCR9-contact residues from Ma et al. 2016 (B. rapa eSRK9-SCR9 crystal structure, PDB 5GYY) are mapped to LEPA alignment columns and overlaid as independent structural validation. The 66 canonical LEPA HV columns are written to SRK_LEPA_HV_positions.tsv as the canonical input for downstream analyses. In the current post-QC dataset, the cross-genus HV-overlap permutation is no longer significant for LEPA pairs (LEPA ↔︎ Brassica p = 0.18; LEPA ↔︎ Arabidopsis p = 0.16); Brassica ↔︎ Arabidopsis remains highly significant (p < 0.0001) — interpreted as drift-eroded standing variation at LEPA HV columns. Rebuild chain (re-run every time Step 10 re-runs): srk_fetch_reference_alleles.py → pad_representatives.py → mafft --add → srk_brassica_hv_mapping.py → then srk_variability_landscape.py.

  • Step 26b — Allele Hypothesis Testing and Crossing Design (srk_allele_hypotheses.py): reads SRK_LEPA_HV_positions.tsv (overrides any internal HV scan), computes HV-only pairwise distances on those columns, detects phylogenetic classes by UPGMA clustering (Class II = Allele_055 in the current dataset), builds the synonymy network (8 named synonymy groups of HV-identical alleles + Synonymy_test bridges; 27 effective bins from 58 defined alleles), and generates the Incompatible / Synonymy_test / Compatible_within / Compatible_cross crossing plan.

  • Step 26c — Synonymy-Group Collapse Test (srk_wgroup_collapse_test.py, optional diagnostic): asks how much of LEPA’s low Shannon entropy is attributable to synonymy-group sequence redundancy (multiple HV-identical alleles) versus genuine residue impoverishment.

  • Step 26d — Per-BL Entropy Decomposition (srk_perBL_entropy_test.py, optional diagnostic): partitions LEPA HV-column residue diversity across the five BLs and compares to Brassica + Arabidopsis dominant residues, distinguishing drift on a shared LEPA ancestral pool (every BL fixes the same Synonymy group 1 alleles independently) from pan-Brassicaceae selection convergence. The verdict in the current dataset: drift, not selection convergence.

  • Step 26e — Hypothesis-Testing Cross Plan Generator (srk_cross_plan.py): translates the Step 26b categorisation into a phased experimental protocol (H0 SI validation; H1a within-Class baseline; H1b between-Class baseline; H2 synonymy-bin tests; H3 hidden-bin tests via heterozygous donors + paired controls).

The current dataset contains 335 ingroup individuals; the AAAA-genotype subset is the ideal cross parents because their pollen carries a single unambiguous SCR identity, making cross outcomes directly interpretable.

8.2.2 Biological Rationale

Why detect HV positions cross-species? A single-species sliding-window scan tells us where in LEPA’s alignment the allele bins differ — but it cannot tell us whether those positions correspond to the conserved SI-specificity surface known from Brassica and Arabidopsis, or to Lepidium-specific variability that may not even contact SCR. By applying the same detection method to LEPA, Brassica, and Arabidopsis SRK alleles separately, we can both (i) confirm that LEPA HV regions co-locate with the known Brassicaceae-conserved HV regions, and (ii) flag any LEPA-specific HV signal that may represent novel specificity sites.

Why Shannon entropy as the primary metric? Shannon entropy is the standard information-theoretic measure of column diversity in protein alignments and is comparable across species with very different per-column residue compositions. The pipeline also computes Wu-Kabat variability (\(V = N \cdot k / n_\text{max}\)) as a sanity check; in the current dataset Brassica HV calls are 88% concordant and Arabidopsis 75% concordant under both metrics, while LEPA’s lower 45% concordance reflects its tighter allele bins (most LEPA columns have low diversity, so Wu-Kabat’s denominator behaves differently than entropy).

Why this validation matters. In the current post-QC dataset (2026-05-11), the cross-Brassicaceae HV-overlap permutation tests return contrasting results: LEPA↔︎Brassica obs = 10 cols, p = 0.18 (n.s.); LEPA↔︎Arabidopsis 15 cols, p = 0.16 (n.s.); Brassica↔︎Arabidopsis 43 cols, p < 0.0001 (highly significant). Earlier analyses on a paralog-contaminated dataset (pre-Steps 4b/7b) returned LEPA↔︎Brassica p = 0.0005 and LEPA↔︎Arabidopsis p = 0.0024. The shift to non-significance for LEPA pairs after QC is interpreted as drift-eroded standing variation — the same conclusion drawn from the per-BL entropy decomposition (Step 26d, 73 % LEPA-specific residues). Brassica↔︎Arabidopsis remains highly significant, so the test still has power on genus-spanning conservation. The structural anchor — 11 of 12 mappable Ma 2016 SCR9-contact residues map within or adjacent to LEPA HV regions — carries the validation independently and confirms the entropy scan is detecting the SI-specificity surface.

Phylogenetic class detection — UPGMA clustering of the HV-only pairwise distance matrix automatically detects the natural class structure: the largest gap in the merge-height sequence defines the cut point. In the current dataset (2026-05-11), this splits 58 allele bins into Class I (57 alleles) and Class II (1 allele, Allele_055) — corresponding to the known phylogenetic lineages in Brassica SRK. Class II alleles are guaranteed to be compatible with all Class I alleles, providing unambiguous Compatible_cross controls at the maximum possible HV distance.

Four-tier cross design: the WITHIN_CLASS_THRESHOLD (default 0.04, approximately the median within-class HV distance) divides within-class pairs into three tiers. The N tier — small but non-zero HV differences — is the scientific core: an incompatible Synonymy_test cross means the two allele bins share recognition specificity (merge them); a compatible Synonymy_test cross confirms the small HV difference is functionally real (keep bins separate). In the current dataset, 6 365 Synonymy_test pairs are available for testing — the largest tier and the highest priority for the crossing experiment.

8.2.3 Bioinformatics

  • Scripts: srk_variability_landscape.py (Step 26a), srk_allele_hypotheses.py (Step 26b)
  • Helper scripts:
    • srk_fetch_reference_alleles.py — one-time NCBI fetch of Brassica + Arabidopsis SRK references (re-run only if reference set changes)
    • pad_representatives.py — pads LEPA representatives (Step 10a output) to uniform length for mafft --add; re-run every time Step 10a re-runs
    • srk_brassica_hv_mapping.py — maps Ma 2016 SCR9-contact residues to LEPA coordinates; re-run every time the combined alignment is rebuilt (column coordinates shift)
  • Environment: Python 3 with numpy, pandas, matplotlib, scipy, biopython, plus mafft on PATH
  • Execution (run from project root):
# (Once per project) fetch external references
python3 srk_fetch_reference_alleles.py

# Rebuild chain — run every time Step 10a re-runs (new library, new N_ALLELES, filter change):
python3 pad_representatives.py            # pads new LEPA reps to uniform length
mafft --add all_reference_SRKs_dedup.fasta SRK_protein_allele_representatives_padded.fasta \
      > SRK_combined_alignment.fasta      # rebuilds 90-sequence combined alignment
python3 srk_brassica_hv_mapping.py        # remaps SCR9 contacts to new LEPA coordinates

# Step 26a — variability landscape
python3 srk_variability_landscape.py

# Step 26b — allele hypothesis testing and crossing design
python3 srk_allele_hypotheses.py

Why the rebuild chain is non-optional: the LEPA allele representatives define the columns of the combined alignment that downstream Step 26 scripts treat as canonical (HV column indices in SRK_LEPA_HV_positions.tsv, SCR9-contact mappings in SRK_brassica_hv_mapping.tsv). Re-running Step 10a with a different N_ALLELES produces a different representative set, shifting mafft --add column coordinates. Skipping the rebuild would silently mix Step 26 outputs against incompatible coordinate systems — a hard-to-debug failure mode. Always rebuild in this order: pad → mafft –add → hv_mapping → Step 26a.

8.2.3.1 Key Parameters

Step 26a — srk_variability_landscape.py:

Parameter Default Description
DOMAIN_REGION (31, 430) S-domain alignment columns; must match Step 10
WINDOW_SIZE 20 Sliding-window width for entropy smoothing
PEAK_SD_FACTOR 1.0 Threshold = mean + k × SD of smoothed Shannon entropy
MIN_HV_RUN 3 Minimum consecutive HV columns to call a region (drops singletons)
PERM_N 10 000 Number of permutations for HV-overlap significance test

Step 26b — srk_allele_hypotheses.py:

Parameter Default Description
DOMAIN_REGION (31, 430) S-domain alignment columns; must match Step 10
LEPA_HV_POSITIONS_FILE SRK_LEPA_HV_positions.tsv Canonical HV columns from Step 26a (overrides legacy internal scan)
WITHIN_CLASS_THRESHOLD 0.04 HV distance boundary between Synonymy_test and Compatible_within
N_GROUPS None Override auto-detected class count
DISTANCE_THRESHOLD None Override auto-detected UPGMA cut height
CROSS_TSV None Set to cross results filename to activate Step 27

8.2.3.2 Input Files

File From Description
SRK_combined_alignment.fasta mafft –add LEPA + Brassica + Arabidopsis SRK references (input to Step 26a)
SRK_brassica_hv_mapping.tsv helper script Ma 2016 SCR9-contact residues → LEPA columns (input to Step 26a)
SRK_protein_allele_representatives.fasta Step 10 LEPA aligned representatives (input to Step 26b)
SRK_individual_zygosity.tsv Step 12 Per-individual tetraploid genotype pattern (input to Step 26b)
SRK_individual_allele_table.tsv Step 11 Individual → allele table (input to Step 26b)
SRK_LEPA_HV_positions.tsv Step 26a Canonical HV columns (input to Step 26b)

8.2.3.3 Output Files

Step 26a outputs:

File Content
SRK_variability_landscape.pdf / figures/SRK_variability_landscape.png Multi-panel: per-species smoothed Shannon entropy + per-species HV tracks + Ma 2016 SCR9-contact residues
SRK_HV_regions_per_species.tsv LEPA / Brassica / Arabidopsis HV runs (start–end, length, threshold)
SRK_LEPA_HV_positions.tsv 66 canonical LEPA HV columns (consumed by Step 26b)
SRK_HV_overlap_permutation.tsv Pairwise HV-overlap permutation tests (3 pairs, p-values, null mean ± SD)

Step 26b outputs:

File Content
SRK_HV_allele_distances.tsv Pairwise HV-only p-distances for all 58 allele bin pairs (computed on the 66 canonical HV cols)
SRK_allele_similarity_heatmap.pdf 63 × 63 HV similarity matrix ordered by UPGMA
SRK_functional_allele_groups.tsv One row per allele bin: class assignment, AAAA count, cross power
SRK_synonymy_candidates.tsv All within-class allele pairs with HV distance > 0, ranked by distance, with testability flag
SRK_AAAA_cross_design_HV.tsv All AAAA × AAAA pairs ranked by category (Incompatible, Synonymy_test, Compatible_within, Compatible_cross) with HV distances and expected outcomes
SRK_cross_design_summary.pdf Three-panel figure: HV distance distribution; cross design schematic; Synonymy_test cross interpretation decision tree
SRK_HV_cluster_figure.pdf UPGMA dendrogram of allele bins coloured by class; AAAA individual count per bin
SRK_synonymy_network_groups.pdf HV-identical synonymy groups (individual alleles, node size ∝ individuals observed); isolated alleles with no HV-identical partner
SRK_synonymy_network_tests.pdf Condensed super-node graph showing N-connectivity between synonymy groups
SRK_synonymy_groups.csv Per-allele synonymy group membership table

8.2.3.4 Key Output Statistics (current dataset, 2026-05-11: 335 individuals, 58 allele bins, 211 AAAA, 66 canonical HV cols)

Cross category Definition Pair count Priority
Incompatible HV-identical (d = 0) 9 700 Negative control — confirms allele identity
Synonymy_test 0 < d < 0.04, same class 10 343 Synonymy test — highest scientific priority
Compatible_within d ≥ 0.04, same class 2 112 Within-class positive control
Compatible_cross Different class 0 (AAAA only) Between-class control — requires heterozygous Allele_055 carrier

Allele bin availability (current dataset, 2026-05-11): 20 bins with full AAAA representation, 9 bins with singleton AAAA, 29 bins with no AAAA (require AAAB/AABB donors). Synonymy network collapses 58 alleles into 8 synonymy groups + 19 isolated singletons = 27 effective bins (39 of 58 alleles fall into synonymy groups, 19 are HV-unique).

8.2.3.5 Output File Descriptions

SRK_functional_allele_groups.tsv

Column Description
Allele Allele bin name
Class Phylogenetic class (Class_I or Class_II)
N_AAAA Number of AAAA individuals carrying this allele
CrossPower full (≥ 2 AAAA), singleton (1 AAAA), none (no AAAA)

SRK_AAAA_cross_design_HV.tsv

Column Description
Mother / Father Individual plant IDs
Mother_allele / Father_allele Allele bin carried by each plant
Mother_class / Father_class Phylogenetic class of each allele
HV_distance Pairwise HV-only p-distance between the two alleles
Category Incompatible, Synonymy_test, Compatible_within, or Compatible_cross — key column for cross prioritisation
Expected Plain-language prediction for seed yield

SRK_synonymy_groups.csv

Column Description
Allele Allele bin name
Synonymy_group Synonymy group 1 through Synonymy group 9 for HV-identical clusters; Isolated for alleles with no HV-identical partner
Group_size Number of allele bins in this synonymy group (1 for isolated alleles)
Obs_count Number of individuals (all genotypes) observed carrying this specific allele
AAAA_count Number of AAAA individuals carrying this allele (cross-design availability)
Group_obs_total Total individuals observed across all alleles in the group — indicates the combined breeding relevance of the group

8.2.4 Scientific Applications

  • Cross prioritisation: Synonymy_test pairs are the highest scientific priority — incompatibility confirms synonymy (merge bins); compatibility confirms the HV difference changes recognition specificity (retain bins)
  • Allele bin refinement: compatible Synonymy_test crosses → bin boundary is real; incompatible Synonymy_test crosses → merge bins into one functional allele
  • Class validation: Compatible_cross crosses (requiring AAAB parents with Allele_055) provide maximum-distance compatible controls anchoring the upper end of the seed yield scale
  • Conservation breeding: Incompatible crosses yielding seeds are direct evidence of SI breakdown — Stage 5 Compatibility Collapse Cascade events — and identify individuals for immediate priority in inter-EO allele rescue crosses
  • Extending the design: adding AAAB parents unlocks the 29 allele bins currently without AAAA representatives and provides the Compatible_cross controls absent from the AAAA-only dataset

8.3 Hypothesis-Testing Cross Plan (Step 26e)

Requires SRK_AAAA_cross_design_HV.tsv, SRK_synonymy_groups.csv, SRK_HV_allele_distances.tsv, and SRK_functional_allele_groups.tsv from Step 26b; SRK_individual_BL_assignments.tsv from Step 13; SRK_individual_zygosity.tsv from Step 12; SRK_individual_allele_table.tsv from Step 11. Run after Step 26b has completed.

8.3.1 Overview

Step 26e is the operational deliverable of Phase 5: it translates the bioinformatics-predicted compatibility categories (Incompatible / Synonymy_test / Compatible_within / Compatible_cross) into a phased experimental protocol that field biologists can execute. The plan structures crossing experiments around five nested hypotheses (H0–H3) that progressively validate the SI system, calibrate the seed-yield scale, test bin-boundary predictions, and extend coverage to “hidden” bins lacking homozygous representatives. Each cross row in the output TSVs is fully traceable — Step 26e never invents pairings; it only translates upstream pipeline outputs into actionable experimental designs.

8.3.2 Rationale — why this step is critical

The cross-design table from Step 26b (SRK_AAAA_cross_design_HV.tsv) lists 13 366 candidate AAAA × AAAA pairs categorised by sequence prediction (Incompatible / Synonymy_test / Compatible_within / Compatible_cross). But not every candidate is interpretable: Synonymy_test crosses where one parent is heterozygous would be confounded by polyploid pollen segregation, and 29 of the 63 sequence bins lack any AAAA representative at all. Step 26e enforces the genotype constraints required to produce clean, interpretable tests of each sequence-based prediction — and packages them into a coherent experimental sequence.

Without Step 26e, two reviewer-facing problems remain:

  1. Interpretability — without explicit genotype constraints, the predicted compatibility categories cannot be tested unambiguously. Heterozygous-donor crosses produce mixed-specificity pollen (e.g., AAAB → 50 % AA + 50 % AB pollen), so seed yields cannot be attributed to a single specificity unless paired controls are provided.
  2. Traceability — every cross in the experimental protocol must be defensible from the underlying bioinformatics. Step 26e generates per-cross provenance (which sequence bin, which HV distance, which Class, which synonymy group, which mother/father genotype, which paired control) so any reviewer can audit any cross row back to a specific upstream output file.

8.3.3 The two-axis decision framework

Every candidate cross is assigned to a hypothesis level by combining two independent axes of evidence:

Axis 1 — Sequence-based cross category (from Step 26a + 26b):

Evidence Source script Source file Threshold rationale
Hypervariable (HV) columns identifying the SI-recognition surface srk_variability_landscape.py SRK_LEPA_HV_positions.tsv Shannon entropy > mean + 1×SD on a 20 aa smoothed profile, with min run of 3 cols. Anchored structurally by 11 of 12 mappable SCR9-contact residues from the Ma et al. 2016 B. rapa eSRK9–SCR9 crystal structure (PDB 5GYY). In the current post-QC dataset (2026-05-11), the cross-genus HV-overlap permutation is no longer significant for LEPA pairs (LEPA↔︎Brassica p = 0.18; LEPA↔︎Arabidopsis p = 0.16); Brassica↔︎Arabidopsis remains highly significant (p < 0.0001) — interpretation: drift-eroded LEPA standing variation.
Pairwise HV distance srk_allele_hypotheses.py Part 2 SRK_HV_allele_distances.tsv p-distance computed only on the 66 canonical HV columns (not the full S-domain)
Phylogenetic class assignment (Class I / Class II) srk_allele_hypotheses.py Part 2 (UPGMA) SRK_functional_allele_groups.tsv Auto-detected at the largest gap in UPGMA merge heights (current data: 0.087 → 0.96; corresponds to the documented Brassicaceae Class I / Class II split)
Synonymy group membership srk_allele_hypotheses.py Part 3c SRK_synonymy_groups.csv Connected components of the graph where allele pairs are linked by HV distance = 0
Cross category per AAAA pair srk_allele_hypotheses.py Part 3 SRK_AAAA_cross_design_HV.tsv Decision tree: different Class → Compatible_cross; HV = 0 → Incompatible; 0 < HV < 0.04 → Synonymy_test; HV ≥ 0.04 → Compatible_within. The 0.04 threshold ≈ median within-class HV distance

Axis 2 — Genotype-based feasibility (introduced in this step):

Evidence Source File Why it matters for hypothesis assignment
Per-individual genotype (AAAA / AAAB / AABB / AABC / ABCD) Step 12 SRK_individual_zygosity.tsv (Genotype column) Determines whether the individual carries unambiguous specificity (AAAA only) or requires polyploid pollen-segregation accounting (AAAB and higher)
Per-individual allele composition Step 12 SRK_individual_zygosity.tsv (Allele_composition column) Authoritative source for which allele is the major (3-copy) vs minor (1-copy) in heterozygous genotypes. Step 26e reads this string directly, not the per-protein SRK_individual_allele_table.tsv
BL membership Step 13 SRK_individual_BL_assignments.tsv Tiebreaker: between-BL pairings preferred (genetic-diversity benefit)

The combination matrix — every cross row is the result of one cell in this table:

Cross category (Axis 1) AAAA × AAAA (clean test) AAAA × heterozygous (carrier required)
Incompatible (HV = 0, same Class) H0 excluded — heterozygous father has ambiguous specificity
Compatible_within (HV ≥ 0.04, same Class) H1a H3 (when AAAA mother + heterozygous donor of hidden bin; requires paired AAAA × AAAA control)
Compatible_cross (different Class) impossible — no AAAA in Class II H1b (AAAA Class I × heterozygous Class II carrier)
Synonymy_test (0 < HV < 0.04, same Class) H2 deferred — AAAA × AAAA is cleaner

8.3.4 The five hypotheses

Level Question Maternal genotype Paternal genotype Allele constraint Why this combination
H0 Does SI rejection actually work? AAAA AAAA both alleles in the same synonymy group (incl. same allele) AAAA × AAAA gives unambiguous SI specificity on both sides — pistil expresses one identity, all pollen carries one identity. Any seed set is direct evidence of SI breakdown (Stage 5 Compatibility Collapse Cascade)
H1a What is the within-Class compatible-cross seed-yield baseline? AAAA AAAA alleles in different synonymy groups, NO Synonymy_test edge (HV ≥ 0.04, same Class) Both genotypes unambiguous; predicted compatible. Anchors the upper baseline of seed yield within Class I
H1b What is the between-Class (max) seed-yield baseline? AAAA Class I Heterozygous (AAAB / AABB / AABC) carrier of a Class II allele mother allele ≠ any of father’s other Class I alleles Class II has zero AAAA carriers in the LEPA dataset, so AAAA × AAAA is impossible. Heterozygous father gives mixed pollen but most carries the Class II specificity (AABB → 17 % AA / 67 % AB / 17 % BB; 84 % of pollen carries Class II)
H2 Do bins separated by small HV differences correspond to distinct functional specificities? AAAA AAAA alleles in different synonymy groups, WITH Synonymy_test edge (0 < HV < 0.04, same Class) Same genotype rigour as H0. The most informative test in the entire plan: 0 seeds → MERGE the two synonymy groups (synonymous specificities); H1a-baseline yield → bin boundary is functionally real
H3 Do the 29 bins lacking AAAA representatives follow the same compatibility hierarchy? AAAA carrier of an allele M known to be Compatible_within with the father’s main allele Heterozygous (AAAB / AABB) carrier of the hidden allele B M ≠ father’s main allele AND M ≠ hidden allele; requires paired AAAA × AAAA control Mother AAAA so pistil specificity is unambiguous; father heterozygous because the hidden bin doesn’t exist as AAAA. The paired control measures the AA-pollen baseline so the hidden allele’s effect can be isolated

8.3.5 Polyploid pollen-segregation accounting

Heterozygous donors (used in H1b and H3) require explicit accounting for tetraploid meiosis because each pollen grain is diploid (2 of 4 allele copies). The script’s interpretation logic uses the C(4,2) = 6 combination rule:

Father genotype Diploid pollen distribution Implication
AAAA (4×A) 100 % AA gametes All pollen carries one specificity (clean test)
AAAB (3×A, 1×B) 50 % AA + 50 % AB gametes AB gametes carry both specificities; pistil rejects if either matches
AABB (2×A, 2×B) 17 % AA + 67 % AB + 17 % BB gametes Mostly AB pollen — usable for H1b when B is the Class II allele (84 % of pollen carries Class II specificity)
AABC (2×A, 1×B, 1×C) 17 % AA + 33 % AB + 33 % AC + 17 % BC Diverse-specificity gametes

H3 explicitly compares total seed yield in the heterozygous-donor cross to a paired AAAA × AAAA control with the same maternal plant: total ≈ paired-control yield → hidden allele incompatible with mother; total ≈ 2 × paired-control yield → hidden allele compatible (additive yield from AB gametes).

8.3.6 Assumptions made explicit (for reviewer-facing justification)

  1. The 66 HV columns are the SI-recognition surface. Justified by structural overlap with the Ma 2016 B. rapa eSRK9–SCR9 crystal structure (PDB 5GYY, 11 of 12 contact residues map within or adjacent to LEPA HV regions). In the current cleaner post-QC dataset (2026-05-11), the cross-Brassicaceae HV-overlap permutation test is no longer significant for LEPA pairs (LEPA ↔︎ Brassica p = 0.18; LEPA ↔︎ Arabidopsis p = 0.16) but Brassica ↔︎ Arabidopsis remains highly significant (p < 0.0001) — the LEPA HV signal is interpreted as drift-eroded standing variation on a shared LEPA ancestral pool (see Step 26d). The structural anchor from Ma 2016 carries the validation.
  2. HV-only p-distance approximates SI-specificity divergence. This is the working hypothesis the H2 synonymy tests will empirically validate or refute — it is not assumed correct, it is the thing being tested.
  3. The UPGMA largest-gap cut correctly identifies Class I vs Class II. Validated by the strongly bimodal HV-distance distribution and by consistency with the documented Brassicaceae class split.
  4. WITHIN_CLASS_THRESHOLD = 0.04 separates small from substantial HV differences. Empirical, derived from the within-class HV-distance distribution. Sensitivity analysis is straightforward (re-run Step 26b with a different value).
  5. AAAA × AAAA crosses give unambiguous specificity assignment. Direct consequence of polyploid SI biology: AAAA individuals carry one specificity in pistil and four identical copies in pollen.
  6. Polyploid pollen segregation follows the C(4,2) = 6 combination rule. Standard tetraploid meiotic assumption. H1b and H3 cross interpretations explicitly account for the AA / AB / BB pollen distributions per genotype.
  7. Heterozygous-donor seed yields are interpretable only when paired with AAAA × AAAA baseline controls. This is why H3 includes paired controls — without them, the AAAB-pollen yield is confounded by the AA-pollen baseline.

8.3.7 Bioinformatics

  • Script: srk_cross_plan.py
  • Environment: Python 3 with numpy, pandas, matplotlib, scipy, biopython
  • Execution (run from project root, after Step 26b):
python3 srk_cross_plan.py

8.3.7.1 Key Parameters (top of script)

Parameter Default Description
WITHIN_CLASS_THRESHOLD 0.04 Must match Step 26b
REPLICATES {H0:9, H1a:9, H1b:9, H2:15, H3:15} Recommended replicates per cross (3 mothers × 3 flowers for known outcomes; 5 × 3 for uncertain Synonymy_test / hidden-bin tests)
N_CROSSES dict, see script Per-phase caps (e.g., H0_within_allele = 5, H1a_far = 10, H2_per_pair = 1, H3_per_bin = 1)

8.3.7.2 Input Files

File From Description
SRK_AAAA_cross_design_HV.tsv Step 26b Pre-computed cross category for all AAAA × AAAA pairs
SRK_synonymy_groups.csv Step 26b Synonymy-group membership per allele
SRK_HV_allele_distances.tsv Step 26b Full 63 × 63 HV-distance matrix
SRK_functional_allele_groups.tsv Step 26b Allele → Class I / Class II
SRK_individual_BL_assignments.tsv Step 13 BL per individual
SRK_individual_zygosity.tsv Step 12 Genotype and Allele_composition per individual
SRK_individual_allele_table.tsv Step 11 Per-protein allele assignments (cross-checked against Allele_composition)

8.3.7.3 Output Files

File Content
SRK_cross_plan_H0_SI_validation.tsv H0 — per-cross row: Mother / Father IDs, genotypes, alleles, BL pair, predicted Incompatible, replicate count, decision rule
SRK_cross_plan_H1a_within_class_baseline.tsv H1a — within-Class compatible-baseline crosses (max-HV-divergent AAAA × AAAA)
SRK_cross_plan_H1b_between_class_baseline.tsv H1b — between-Class baseline (Compatible_cross via heterozygous Class II carrier)
